text.title: Django Testing Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Missing django_db marker, over-mocking ORM, patching import target not use site, and transaction=True overuse.

antipattern.description: Writing a test function that accesses the database without the `@pytest.mark.django_db` marker.
antipattern.reason: Without the marker, pytest-django blocks all DB access. The test fails with a confusing `Failed: Database access not allowed` error that looks like a setup problem. Always add `@pytest.mark.django_db` to any test that creates or reads model instances.

antipattern.description: Adding `@pytest.mark.django_db` to every test in a module via a class-level mark instead of using `pytestmark = pytest.mark.django_db` at module level.
antipattern.reason: Repeating the mark on every function is mechanical and forgotten when new tests are added. Module-level `pytestmark` applies to all tests in the module. Use it for test modules that are exclusively DB tests.

example.note: Module-level django_db mark for a DB-heavy test module
example.code:
import pytest
pytestmark = pytest.mark.django_db # Applies to all tests in this module
def test_create_user(user_factory): user = user_factory(email="test@example.com") assert user.id is not None
def test_user_is_active_by_default(user_factory): user = user_factory() assert user.is_active

antipattern.description: Patching `Model.objects.filter` or `Model.objects.get` with `MagicMock` to avoid DB access in service tests.
antipattern.reason: Mocking the ORM tests the mock, not the code. The mock always returns what you tell it, so the test passes even if the service has a broken queryset. Real DB tests with `@pytest.mark.django_db` and savepoint rollback are fast enough — use them. Over-mocked tests also break when QuerySet chaining order changes.

example.note: Mocking ORM — tests the mock, not the service
Real DB test — tests actual SQL behavior
example.code: @pytest.mark.django_db def test_get_active_users(user_factory): active = user_factory(is_active=True) inactive = user_factory(is_active=False) result = get_active_user_ids() assert active.id in result assert inactive.id not in result

section.title: Patching Import Target vs Use Site

antipattern.description: Patching `apps.emails.utils.send_mail` instead of `apps.orders.services.send_mail` — patching where the function is defined rather than where it is imported and used.
antipattern.reason: Python's `patch()` replaces a name in a module's namespace. If `orders.services` does `from emails.utils import send_mail`, the reference in `orders.services` is already bound. Patching `emails.utils.send_mail` has no effect on the already-bound reference. Patch the use site: `patch('apps.orders.services.send_mail')`.

example.note: Patch the use site, not the definition site
example.code: # orders/services.py: from emails.utils import send_mail # Correct: patch where it's used @patch("apps.orders.services.send_mail") def test_order_confirmation_sends_email(mock_send, order_factory): order = order_factory(status="pending") confirm_order(order) mock_send.assert_called_once_with(order.user.email, subject=ANY)

antipattern.description: Using `@pytest.mark.django_db(transaction=True)` on all tests as a default to "be safe" instead of only for tests that specifically test `on_commit` behavior.
antipattern.reason: `transaction=True` requires full table truncation after each test instead of savepoint rollback — 5-10x slower. A test suite of 500 tests that all use `transaction=True` adds minutes to the CI run for no benefit. Use it only for code paths that rely on `connection.on_commit()`, post-transaction signals, or Celery task visibility.
